does: This paper introduces KSAFE-MM, a comprehensive multimodal safety benchmark designed to evaluate vulnerabilities in multimodal large language models within Korean cultural contexts. The benchmark consists of two complementary components: KSAFE-MM-G, which adapts generic safety risks into linguistically contextualized Korean scenarios, and KSAFE-MM-C, which targets culture-specific vulnerabilities using localized visual queries paired with adversarial textual prompts. The evaluation framework systematically covers both universal safety concerns and Korean-cultural-dependent risks by combining visual inputs from authentic local contexts with jailbreak-style text queries, enabling comprehensive assessment of how MLLMs respond to multimodal safety challenges that reflect real-world cultural particulars rather than generic English-centric scenarios.
connects: This work addresses critical gaps in the existing MLLM safety evaluation landscape, which has predominantly focused on English-language benchmarks and generic risks disconnected from local cultures. It builds on prior safety evaluation efforts for large language models while extending them to the multimodal domain and non-English contexts. The paper responds to growing recognition that safety vulnerabilities vary significantly across cultural, linguistic, and visual contexts—a limitation acknowledged in recent work on culturally-aware AI evaluation. By combining linguistic contextualization strategies with localized visual grounding, KSAFE-MM connects to broader research on culturally-aware NLP and multimodal understanding, while drawing from adversarial robustness and jailbreak-testing methodologies developed for language models.
enables: This benchmark establishes foundational infrastructure for culturally-grounded multimodal safety research beyond English, opening pathways for developing and evaluating MLLMs that robustly handle cultural-specific risks. The general-to-local evaluation framework can be adapted for other languages and cultures, establishing a template for localized safety benchmarking across diverse populations. By identifying Korean-specific vulnerabilities in vision-language systems, the work enables future research into mitigation strategies that account for cultural context, training methods that improve multimodal safety in non-Western settings, and evaluation frameworks that recognize how safety requirements differ globally. It also highlights the need for culturally-informed model design and creates opportunities for similar benchmarking efforts in other underrepresented languages and cultures.
